---
title: "How to Play"
description: "Controls, the objective, and the moment-to-moment battle loop."
---

You command the [**blue army**]{.army-blue} (top of the field). Your objective:
defeat the [**red army**]{.army-red} (bottom). A side loses when it has no units
left fighting --- every remaining unit is dead or routing.

```{=html}
<figure class="demo">
  <video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
         poster="media/clash.jpg">
    <source src="media/clash.mp4" type="video/mp4">
    Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
    <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
  </video>
  <figcaption>Selecting units and issuing move/attack orders during a battle.</figcaption>
</figure>
```

## Controls

| Action | Control |
| --- | --- |
| Select a unit | **Left-click** its block or its raised flag |
| Select multiple | **Left-click and drag** a box |
| Move | **Right-click** the ground |
| Form up | **Right-click and drag** open ground (left flank → right flank); with several units selected, the line is split among them |
| Form up in selection order | **Shift + right-click and drag** (instead of the default left-to-right field order) |
| Form-up split mode | <kbd>Y</kbd> cycles through enabled modes (equal-depth / equal-width); configure which modes cycle and which is the battle default in **☰ Menu** |
| Reform before move | **☰ Menu** or **control bar** toggles whether units pause to settle their ranks before marching (on by default) |
| Walk advance | **☰ Menu → Walk advance** keeps units at walk speed throughout --- no jog or sprint phase. Required for formed stances (shield wall, pike phalanx) that break on a run |
| Attack | **Right-click** an enemy unit |
| Group attack mode | <kbd>X</kbd> cycles focused ↔ distributed · or **control bar** |
| Add waypoint | **Shift + Right-click** (chain several move points) |
| Order stance | <kbd>H</kbd> hold · <kbd>F</kbd> flank · <kbd>R</kbd> rear · <kbd>K</kbd> skirmish · <kbd>G</kbd> support · <kbd>Esc</kbd> clear |
| Formation | <kbd>T</kbd> (cycles Normal / Tight / Loose) · or **control bar** |
| Line width | <kbd>[</kbd> narrower · <kbd>]</kbd> wider, or **drag a flank grip** |
| Drill turn in place | <kbd>V</kbd> about-face (180°) · <kbd>Q</kbd> quarter-turn left · <kbd>E</kbd> quarter-turn right |
| Wheel (hinge on a flank) | <kbd>Z</kbd> wheel left · <kbd>C</kbd> wheel right (swings 90° about the standing flank file) |
| Merge selected units | <kbd>M</kbd> |
| Control groups | <kbd>Ctrl</kbd> + <kbd>0</kbd>-<kbd>9</kbd> bind · <kbd>0</kbd>-<kbd>9</kbd> recall |
| Distance legend | **☰ Menu → Distance legend** toggles the map-scale bar (bottom-right, on by default) |
| Order distance labels | **☰ Menu → Order distance labels** toggles the metric distance shown on each order line in the hold-<kbd>Space</kbd> survey (on by default) |
| Unit speed labels | **☰ Menu → Unit speed labels** shows each unit's current speed in metres/second above it in the hold-<kbd>Space</kbd> survey (off by default) |
| Pan camera | <kbd>W</kbd><kbd>A</kbd><kbd>S</kbd><kbd>D</kbd> / arrow keys / screen edges |
| Zoom | **Mouse wheel** |
| Show all orders + formation preview | hold <kbd>Space</kbd> |
| Active pause | <kbd>P</kbd> (or <kbd>Shift</kbd> + <kbd>Space</kbd>) |
| All shortcuts (this list, in-game) | <kbd>?</kbd> (Shift + <kbd>/</kbd>) or **☰ Menu → Shortcuts** |

A right-click on empty ground is a **move** order; a right-click on an enemy is an
**attack** order. Selection and camera stay live even while paused, so you can plan
under pressure.

**Control bar.** When you have units selected, a control bar appears at the bottom of
the screen with clickable buttons for the most-used unit toggles.
Use it as a mouse-driven alternative to the hotkeys whenever you prefer clicking to typing.

- **Formation** --- click Normal, Tight, or Loose directly (same as <kbd>T</kbd>).
- **Stance** --- click any order stance to arm it for your next right-click order (same as the stance hotkeys; <kbd>Esc</kbd> / the Normal button clears it).
- **Reform** --- toggle Reform before move (same as ☰ Menu → Reform before move).
- **Focused atk / Spread atk** --- cycle the group-attack mode (same as <kbd>X</kbd>).

The bar updates immediately when you select a different unit or change a setting elsewhere.

**Pace modes (AUTO by default).** Units walk on approach, jog when a ranged enemy is
within range (under missile fire), and accelerate to full sprint speed once within
about 200 px of the target --- so a charge arrives at full momentum without wearing
out the troops on the march. Toggle **☰ Menu → Walk advance** to lock units to walk
pace throughout; this is required for formed stances like shield wall or pike phalanx
that break if the unit breaks into a run.

**Move order sequence.** When you right-click a destination, the regiment turns to
face it and marches there in good order: it **pivots about its centre** onto the new
heading --- turning in place before it advances on a sharp turn --- marches, and settles
back into formation on arrival. The next two sections cover the special cases: a small
sideways shift (**side-step**) and a larger turn (**centre pivot**).

**Reform before move (default on).** By default, a fresh move order makes a unit hold
its position briefly (~0.8 s) while its ranks settle into formation before it steps
off. The unit spends this hold **pivoting in place** toward the destination, so its
ranks are already coming onto the new heading before the first step.
To make units move immediately without the pause, uncheck
**☰ Menu → Reform before move** or click **Reform** in the control bar. The setting is
baked into each recorded order, so replays reproduce whichever mode was active when
you issued them.

**Side-step (small lateral moves).** A move order that only shifts a unit a short way
to the side --- less than about its own width --- is carried out as a **side-step**: the
unit shuffles sideways at a walk while holding its current facing, instead of pivoting
the whole line round to face the move and then pivoting back. So a small dressing of the
line keeps every soldier looking the same way and keeps the ranks in order. A longer move,
or one that heads forward, still turns to face the destination and marches normally.

```{=html}
<figure class="demo">
  <video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
         poster="media/sidestep.jpg">
    <source src="media/sidestep.mp4" type="video/mp4">
    Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
    <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
  </video>
  <figcaption>Side-step: a short lateral move shuffles the regiment sideways while it holds its facing, rather than pivoting.</figcaption>
</figure>
```

**Centre pivot (larger turns).** A move order that turns a unit toward a new heading makes
it **pivot about its centre** there gradually rather than snapping --- it turns toward its
travel direction in good order as it sets off. When the heading change is sharp, the unit
**pivots before it advances**: it nearly halts, comes onto its new facing, then marches.
For a destination directly **behind** the unit this is a 180° centre pivot --- the whole
regiment turns around to face where it is going and then steps off, instead of sliding
backwards. (That is distinct from the standing **about-face** drill on <kbd>V</kbd> below,
where each soldier turns in place and the block never rotates.) So a unit always marches
facing its destination, and never reverses into it. (A chasing or charging unit still turns
instantly --- the gradual pivot is for your own move orders, where order matters more than
the last fraction of a second.)

```{=html}
<figure class="demo">
  <video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
         poster="media/about_face.jpg">
    <source src="media/about_face.mp4" type="video/mp4">
    Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
    <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
  </video>
  <figcaption>Centre pivot (180&deg;): ordered to a point behind it, the regiment pivots in good order to face the destination, then marches off.</figcaption>
</figure>
```

**About-face in place (conversio, <kbd>V</kbd>).** Press <kbd>V</kbd> with a unit
selected to issue the *conversio*: every soldier reverses 180° where they stand --- the
file/rank grid stays put, the front rank becomes the rear rank man-for-man, and the
unit is now facing the other way. Unlike the gradual **centre pivot** that accompanies a
move order (which rotates the whole block around its centre as it marches), the conversio
is a standing drill command: no one takes a step. Use it to face a threat behind the line
without marching first, or to quickly reorient a reserve before it moves up.

**Quarter-turn in place (<kbd>Q</kbd> / <kbd>E</kbd>).** Press <kbd>Q</kbd> to turn the
selected unit a quarter-turn left, or <kbd>E</kbd> to turn it right: every soldier pivots
90° where they stand. No one marches and the grid of men does not reorganize --- they
simply face the new way, so the block now presents its **short edge** as its frontage
(its frontage and depth swap relative to the facing, without anyone moving). Like the
conversio, it is a standing facing change. Use it to throw a fresh face toward a flank, or
as the first step of marching off to the side in good order.

**Wheel on a flank (circumductio, <kbd>Z</kbd> / <kbd>C</kbd>).** Press <kbd>Z</kbd> to
wheel the selected unit left, or <kbd>C</kbd> to wheel it right. One end of the line ---
the standing flank file --- holds its ground while the rest of the block swings around it
like a door on a hinge, reorienting the whole line 90° while every man keeps his place in
the ranks. Unlike the quarter-turn, this is not a standing facing change: the far flank
actually marches an arc, so the line ends up facing a new direction *and* lying along a
new line. Use it to refuse a flank, to swing a wing forward and take an enemy line in the
side, or to change the front an entire wing presents without breaking its order.

```{=html}
<figure class="demo">
  <video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
         poster="media/wheel.jpg">
    <source src="media/wheel.mp4" type="video/mp4">
    Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
    <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
  </video>
  <figcaption>Wheel (circumductio): the line swings 90&deg; about its standing flank file --- the near flank holds while the far flank marches a wide arc --- reorienting the whole block without breaking its ranks. Wheeled right (C), then back left (Z).</figcaption>
</figure>
```

**Zoom in to see the soldiers.** From the usual overview each regiment is a block of
small marks. Zoom in far enough and those marks become individual figures, and each
type carries its own gear --- spearmen shoulder a spear, archers carry a bow, other
foot raise a shield, and cavalry ride a mount that faces the regiment's march. So you
can watch the ranks as people, not dots, and still read who is who. A small white
facing pip on each figure shows its exact facing --- the figure itself only mirrors
left/right, so the pip is what lets a maneuver's precise turn (a quarter-turn, say)
read correctly even at this zoom, not just a coarse left/right flip. Zoom back out and
they collapse to the legible marks again --- each mark points the way its soldier faces,
so you can read a regiment's facing at a glance even from the overview. The mark shape
tells you the type, too: a dart for spearmen, a kite for archers, and an arrow-pointer
for other foot and cavalry. All three are compact directional glyphs, so a rank reads as
arrows whichever way it faces rather than smearing into bars.

**Reading distances.** A small semi-translucent scale bar sits in the bottom-right corner,
labelled with the real metre distance it spans (e.g. "50 m") --- it rescales as you zoom,
so you can always judge range and reach at a glance. Toggle it off in **☰ Menu → Distance
legend** if you'd rather not see it.

**Order stances.** Beyond plain move/attack you can arm a *stance* with a hotkey
before you click; a coloured cursor shows which is active, and <kbd>Esc</kbd> clears it.

- **Hold** (<kbd>H</kbd>) --- stand ground; don't chase a detected enemy.
- **Attack flank** (<kbd>F</kbd>) / **Attack rear** (<kbd>R</kbd>) --- maneuver to
  strike the enemy's side or back, where hits land far harder
  (see [Tactics](tactics.qmd#order-stances)).
- **Skirmish** (<kbd>K</kbd>) --- a ranged unit keeps its distance, backing off when
  a threat closes instead of standing to fire.
- **Support** (<kbd>G</kbd>) --- right-click a friendly to guard it: the unit shadows
  that ally and engages anything that threatens it.

```{=html}
<figure class="demo">
  <video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
         poster="media/support.jpg">
    <source src="media/support.mp4" type="video/mp4">
    Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
    <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
  </video>
  <figcaption>Support stance: a unit guards an ally, shadowing it and engaging any threat.</figcaption>
</figure>
```

Press <kbd>T</kbd> to cycle a selected unit's **formation** --- *Tight* closes ranks
for better missile and charge defence, *Loose* spreads out. Every unit can cycle
through all three; each type just starts at a different one --- spearmen deploy
Tight (locked shields against a charge), archers deploy Loose (room to fire),
and sword-armed infantry and cavalry deploy at the plain Normal combat order.

**Resize the line.** A regiment normally forms up at a sensible width, but you can
reshape it: press <kbd>]</kbd> to widen the line by one file (shallower, more men
fighting at the front) or <kbd>[</kbd> to narrow it (deeper, fewer up front but more
staying power). With a single unit selected, two small grips appear on its flanks ---
**drag** a grip in or out to set the width directly, with a live preview of the new
file count. The width holds as the unit takes casualties, and shows in the info panel.

**Deploy with a drag (form up).** With a single unit selected, **right-click and drag
across open ground** to lay out where it should stand: the start of the drag is the
unit's **left flank**, the end is its **right flank**. The unit marches over and pivots
to face the line --- the drag sets its position, width, and facing in one gesture, with
a live preview of the front line and a forward arrow. A short right-click (no drag) is
still a plain move.

**Deploy several units at once.** Select multiple units first, then drag the same
flank line: it's split into one contiguous slice per unit, with a small gap between them.
By default the line splits to **equal depth** --- every regiment forms up the same number
of ranks, so a bigger unit simply takes a wider slice and the whole front is a uniform
wall. Press <kbd>Y</kbd> to switch to **equal width** (every unit the same frontage, so a
small unit just stacks deeper) and back; the choice you make in **☰ Menu → Form-up** is the
default each battle starts with. By default the units fill the line **left-to-right by where
they already stand**, so each marches to the nearest slice without crossing a neighbour;
hold **Shift** while you drag to place them in **selection order** instead (the first unit
you picked takes the left flank). The preview shows every unit's slice, file count, and the
active split mode before you release.

**Survey all orders (hold Space).** Holding <kbd>Space</kbd> overlays every unit's
current order on the battlefield: a dashed green line to each move destination, a red
line to each attack target, and a teal link for support duties. At the end of each move
line, a **ghost formation** (faint green dots) shows exactly where each individual
soldier will stand when the regiment finishes its march --- so you can check whether
units will fit, overlap, or leave a gap before they arrive.

Each order line also carries a **distance label** at its midpoint, in the same metres
the scale bar uses --- the remaining march for a move (the full route, summed over any
waypoints), or the range to the target for an attack or support link. The number counts
down as the unit closes. Toggle the labels off in **☰ Menu → Order distance labels**
(on by default).

```{=html}
<figure class="demo">
  <video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
         poster="media/order_distance.jpg">
    <source src="media/order_distance.mp4" type="video/mp4">
    Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
    <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
  </video>
  <figcaption>Order distance labels: the green move line shows the remaining march in metres, counting down as the regiment closes on its target.</figcaption>
</figure>
```

## The battle loop

Here is the cycle in practice, shown with a cavalry charge:

```{=html}
<figure class="demo">
  <video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
         poster="media/charge.jpg">
    <source src="media/charge.mp4" type="video/mp4">
    Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
    <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
  </video>
  <figcaption>Cavalry charge: two horse units receive an attack order and accelerate to full speed on contact.</figcaption>
</figure>
```

1. **Select** --- left-click a unit, or drag a box to grab several at once. The info
   panel fills with the selected unit's stats.
2. **Order** --- right-click to send them. Move them onto an enemy's flank, or
   right-click the enemy directly to attack. There is a brief **reaction delay**
   (~0.5 s, faster for cavalry) before units execute --- orders aren't instant.
3. **Watch the bars** --- each unit flies a small **regimental flag** in its team
   colour and shows a strength bar that drops as it takes casualties. The flag is a
   click target too: clicking it selects the unit, just like clicking the block.
   Morale matters as much as numbers (see [Tactics](tactics.qmd)).
4. **Navigate terrain** --- the field has two terrain patches: a **dark-green forest**
   (slows movement to 60%) on the left flank, and a **tan hill** (impassable) on the
   right. Units route around the hill automatically. See [Tactics → Terrain](tactics.qmd#terrain).
5. **Exploit the break** --- when a unit **routs**, it flees (shown faded) and stops
   counting toward the battle. Routs spread to nearby allies, so a single break can
   cascade.
6. **Win** --- eliminate or rout the entire enemy army and a **Victory** overlay
   appears, with **Fight Again** to restart.

## Campaign Map

Sparta includes a turn-based campaign layer on top of its tactical battles.
Select a scenario from the **Main Menu** to open the campaign map.

**On your turn:**

1. Click one of your provinces (highlighted when you have an army there).
2. Click an adjacent province to move, or attack an enemy one if you are at war.
3. Press **End Turn** when you are done. Each AI faction takes its turn automatically.

**Diplomacy:** the right-hand panel lists every surviving rival. Click **Declare War** or
**Sue for Peace** to change your stance. Suing for peace sets a truce that prevents
re-declaring war for a few turns.

**Rulers and AI personality:** each faction is led by a named ruler whose *trait* shapes
its diplomacy. The turn banner at top-left names the current faction's ruler in
parentheses (for example, Rome's banner reads "Rome (Julius Caesar)").

| Trait | War threshold | Peace threshold |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Normal | Attacks at 1.5× strength advantage | Sues for peace below 0.6× |
| Aggressive | Attacks at 1.25× advantage | Fights longer (sues below 0.33×) |
| Defensive | Needs a 2× advantage to attack | Sues for peace sooner (below 0.8×) |

**Province counts** appear in the standings line below the banner. Own every province
to win the campaign.

## Recording & replays

Every battle is recorded automatically --- a `● REC` indicator shows top-center. When
a battle ends it's saved, and you can:

- **Watch Replay** --- re-run the battle you just played (`▶ REPLAY`). You can still
  pan and inspect units during playback, but can't issue new orders.
- **Load Replay** --- open any earlier saved battle from a file picker (top-right,
  also on the end screen).
- **Fight Again** --- start a fresh, newly-recorded battle.

See **[Replays](replays.qmd)** for how the deterministic system works and why the
logs are tiny.

---

*Source: [`README.md`](https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta/blob/main/README.md)
in the repository.*
